metadata.title: Artificial Intelligence in Games (Champandard) — Book Review
metadata.color_semantics: claude fidelity: INK #3D3929; SPARK #D97757 one accent.
metadata.author: Seth Brown
metadata.note: HAI Fellows batch v2 — Teardown register, @NikBearBrown, Remotion animated bookends. Credit: Seth Brown — Humanitarians AI Fellow. HONESTY: all numbers trace to source article.

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. Game AI must hold 60fps, a hard computational constraint that academic AI never has to face. Success is the illusion of intelligence: believable to the specific player, regardless of underlying sophistication. Reynolds' 1987 flocking decomposes into three simple, independently implementable forces: Separation, Alignment, and Cohesion. Each force is buildable on its own, though the book skips the exponential scaling problem.

artifactLines: Game AI must hold 60fps, a hard computational constraint that academic AI never has to face.
Success is the illusion of intelligence: believable to the specific player, regardless of underlying sophistication.
Reynolds' 1987 flocking decomposes into three simple, independently implementable forces: Separation, Alignment, and Cohesion.
Each force is buildable on its own, though the book skips the exponential scaling problem.

beats.narration_text: The 60fps hard constraint that academic AI never faces reframed game AI for me, and seeing Reynolds' flocking decomposed into Separation, Alignment, and Cohesion made it feel buildable. Help me go deeper: (1) explain how each of the three forces is computed per agent, (2) show why they must run inside a single frame's budget, and (3) unpack the 'illusion of intelligence' as believable to this specific player regardless of real sophistication.
